title: The American age was the human age
subtitle: Reflections on 250 years.
contentHtml:
“What other country would have done this?” — Daniel Inouye

“We don’t repeat this every day, but there are 33 words that are very sacred to all of us. We do the repetition a little differently but ‘We hold these truths to be self-evident. That all men are created equal. That they are endowed by their creator certain inalienable rights. That among these are life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.’ It’s operational. Believe me.” — Daniel Inouye

China’s last imperial dynasty, the Qing, lasted 268 years. The Ming Dynasty that preceded it lasted just slightly longer, at 276, while the celebrated Tang made it to 289. Decades before each of those dynasties officially fell, they were shells of their former selves, with much of the land outside the control of the central government.

Barring abrupt catastrophe, the United States — which today marks a quarter of a millennium — will probably last as long as the Qing, the Ming, and probably the Tang. The country’s foundations are certainly shakier than when I was a child, but we have not yet entered an obvious terminal phase. The economy is still robust — our GDP remains on the smooth upward trend it has been on since we started measuring such things eight decades ago:

We cannot (or will not) build a functional passenger train network, but our AI industry is upending the world. Our health care costs twice as much as that of other rich nations, but our houses are huge and luxurious. Our cities are burdened with crime and disorder, but e-commerce delivers everything we need, straight to our door. The hour of the wolf is not yet upon us. It seems a safe bet that there will still be a United States of America in 2044, 2052, and probably 2065.

And yet we’ve reached the stage where we can peer through the fog and see how this grand experiment might be heading toward its conclusion. Much of the country has eased into a comfortable equilibrium of sclerosis; local veto power either prevents the construction of factories, housing, energy, transportation, and other infrastructure, or delays it by decades, or raises the cost to multiples of what other rich countries pay. The past has become more valuable than the future to many Americans; they cling desperately to the power to enforce stasis, preserving a facade of the country they grew up in at the expense of the very dynamism that made that country great.

That sclerosis seeps into everything else. Immigration, and even migration from city to city, becomes a vicious zero-sum fight over a fixed housing supply. Cities decay into museums of themselves. The industries of the future can only be built in America if they take up nearly no land, use nearly no energy, require very little bank financing, and are able to procure skilled labor as needed from abroad. Somehow the internet industry satisfied all of those conditions for three decades, but that time is done.

Our politics, meanwhile, has degenerated into movements defined more by who they hate than by any positive vision for the country’s future. Rightists are consumed by their hatred for immigration, leftists by their hatred for Israel. Even intellectual liberals — my own movement and social class, if only by process of elimination — increasingly subordinate other goals to their dream of lowering the social status of wealthy technologists.

To the extent that popular visions of a better America exist, they are rank and obvious fantasies — homogeneous harmony that rightists will never be able to create, or socialist plenty that socialism is incapable of delivering. There are plenty of workable, feasible future visions that would advance the frontiers of freedom, dignity, and prosperity; no faction of the engaged American public seems particularly interested in them.

Political discourse in America is still the baleful thing it became in the 2010s — a vicious free-for-all of social media influencers using hatred, division, fear, and misinformation to win the ear of the powerful political staffer, think tank, and journalist classes. Everything exists in the shadow of the almost-revolution of the late 2010s — an upheaval whose force has mostly receded but whose damage has yet to be fully assessed. Meanwhile, the country’s powerful enemies abroad are sharpening their knives.

If there is a reason to be pessimistic about America’s future, it’s that so few of the country’s citizens seem to believe in it. We used to be an unusually patriotic nation; now Americans are less proud of their nation than Europeans, Asians, or people in any other major world region:

The rightists who now dominate the GOP believe that America will only be valuable as a going concern if its old ethnic composition can be forcibly restored. The leftists who are surging among the Democrats, meanwhile, have a vision of America as an evil empire that could have come straight from old Soviet propaganda; this idea finds fertile ground among progressives who for a decade have mainlined the notion that America is “stamped from the beginning” with racism. How will the country be saved if no one thinks it’s something worth saving?

It would be foolish, of course, to predict that the U.S. is headed for the scrap heap within our lifetimes; uncountably many such predictions have made fools of the people who made them. The country is not facing mortal, imminent danger; its enemies are powerful but most of its wounds are self-inflicted. The United States may yet survive, with its territory and its constitutional democracy intact, to its 300th birthday and beyond.

Even so, it’s far from clear what a nation will even mean in those decades and centuries to come. The human race as a whole is set to dwindle, as fertility falls below replacement in every corner of the globe. At the same time, more and more of the thinking done on the planet will be done in data centers rather than within human brains. In that posthuman world, it’s not at all clear that humanity will even need the nation-state to provide the crucial organizing and coordinating role it played during the previous two and a half centuries.

So whether or not this is the beginning of the end for America, it’s the beginning of the end for something even bigger and more important — the human age. By that I mean the age when humanity, unassisted by any higher intelligence, broke free of the chains that had bound it for millennia and became something greater.

For most of its history, the human race existed at near-animal levels of subsistence. The adoption of agriculture — the most important change in human society before the modern age — increased humanity’s numbers and changed the surface of the Earth, but for the most part it did not alter the species’ fundamental poverty. Civilizations would rise and be cast down again and again by the Malthusian Ceiling. Rome, the Han Dynasty, the Abbasid Caliphate, the Song Dynasty, and many others found themselves unable to outrun what seemed like a fundamental fact of the Universe. And so human existence seemed to be a cycle of rise and fall.

Then something happened that had never happened before. Yes, it’s this:

This exploding line is actually two things in one. Industrial technology raised human living standards by about 14x over the two centuries from 1820. It also allowed humanity to escape Malthusian population constraints, allowing an 8x rise in the human population. Humanity became godlike in its powers, blanketing and transforming the globe. And our species achieved this breakout simply by coming up with better ideas — novel ways to organize ourselves, conduct our affairs, and understand the world around us.

That age of human flowering was remarkably coterminous with the age during which the United States of America was the foremost nation upon the face of the Earth. Or one of the two, I should say, since its only rival for that title was Great Britain. The UK gave birth to industrial technology, modern science, physics, economics, computer science, and so on. But even so, America didn’t just scale up European innovations during its first century. It was at the forefront of innovation from the start — Americans created interchangeable parts, mechanized farm machinery, the telegraph, vulcanized rubber, anesthesia, commercial petroleum drilling, and more.

But it was in the late 19th and 20th centuries that America pulled ahead and eclipsed both Great Britain and all other nations of Earth. That happened in economic terms around 1880:

In terms of technological innovation, the switchover happened around the same time. Over the next few decades, Americans would go on to invent (or co-invent) the telephone, the light bulb, electric power and power grids, skyscrapers, electric motors, air conditioning, airplanes, synthetic plastic, assembly lines, television, FM radio, synthetic fibers, the helicopter, and so on. After World War 2, of course, this dominance became absolute, and America — sometimes together with its allies, sometimes not — invented much of what you see around you in the modern world.

What allowed America both to create this tremendous bounty of innovation, and to spread its economic benefits to the average citizen, was its economic system. The U.S. was always a haven of free enterprise, and markets had much to do with why America was both so innovative and so rich. But it wasn’t the only piece of the puzzle; universal public education, massive government support for infrastructure, the modern university, and government promotion of science all played important roles.

It’s not clear how much America’s social and governmental innovations — democracy, constitutionalism, federalism, and so on — played a role in enabling and supporting its economic innovations. That’s a question people will be debating for a very long time. But it’s clear that these ideas have become compelling in their own right — almost every country has a constitution, and even dictatorships try to boost their popularity by calling themselves democracies.

And in the moral realm, the U.S. was almost always at the forefront (depending, of course, on your opinion of what constitutes the “forefront”). Whether it was declaring that “all men are created equal” at its outset, fighting a civil war to end slavery, or pushing the world to accept the idea of universal human rights after World War 2, American ideals often became global ideals with a lag. People also forget how the U.S. pushed European countries — especially the UK, but also France and the Netherlands — to give up their colonial empires, using World War 2 and Cold War assistance as leverage.

It is astonishing that one single nation managed to be at the forefront of the world economically, technologically, institutionally, and morally for well over a century. It is even more astonishing that one nation managed to do all of that during the greatest period of change that humanity had ever known. Wave after wave of wrenching change battered the human race for a quarter of a millennium, and each time, the United States was there, channeling and ultimately mastering the wave before anyone (or almost anyone) else did.

It is common to compare America to great nations or empires of the past. The Rome comparison is the most common, and I started this post by comparing us to imperial China. In fact, these comparisons don’t do justice to what the United States accomplished. Rome, the Song Dynasty, and every other great premodern civilization existed during an essentially static world — the long pause of the agricultural age.

The United States has spent its entire quarter millennium of existence in the chaos following the Industrial Revolution. It survived a 250-year period that saw the end of the British Empire, the Russian Empire, the Qing Dynasty, the Japanese Empire, the French Empire, the Soviet Union, and countless other regimes. But it didn’t just survive for that whole 250 years; it led the world in almost all respects during most of it.

That’s the most incredible performance of any country in human history so far. Even if you count all 5000 years of Chinese history as one single country, just 250 years of America managed to eclipse that totality in many ways.

How did America pull this off? Its populace was large, but smaller than China, India, or the old USSR. It had a lot of natural resources, but the British Empire, Russia, and the Qing Dynasty had just as many. Its people were not more intelligent or harder-working or inherently more moral. Christianity did not produce similar results elsewhere. What was America’s secret sauce?

It’s possible, of course, that the United States simply lucked out from an extended sequence of right-places-at-the-right-times. But although we’ll never know, I suspect that America’s secret sauce was respect for the human individual.

The system of free enterprise that allowed the U.S. economy to flourish was based on the idea of giving individuals free rein to pursue financial gain within the boundaries of the law. The democracy and human rights that the U.S. championed were both based on respect for the individual, and other countries adopted these ideas when they found that their populaces demanded them. Individualism made it difficult for the U.S. to get captured by the kind of totalitarianism that captured Germany, Russia, and many other countries during the turbulent 20th century.

As for that large population, the entire reason that America has a large population is immigration. There has always been a loud minority of Americans who declared that people from poorer countries — “beaten men from beaten races”, as Francis Walker wrote in 1896 — weren’t collectively fit to become Americans, and that their presence would destroy the country. But America’s deep-rooted institutional respect for the individual kept the immigration flowing, which eventually gave the country its large population, large domestic market, deep fiscal pockets, and huge talent pool.

This is why the American age was the human age. The United States represented a living demonstration that if the institutions of society placed the value of the human individual above all else, then individuals would serve society to the best of their abilities. This does not mean that the group or collective was unimportant; it means that the collective is the most powerful when uplifting, empowering, and respecting the individual is its goal.

That, I believe, is the true meaning of “All men are created equal” — the most important 5 words ever written. It does not mean that all humans are equally strong, or smart, or good. It means that the inherent value of a human comes not from their capabilities, but from their individuality. The United States certainly never embodied this ideal to perfection, but the principle did the country, and the world, great service nonetheless.

Whether this principle will continue to produce the best societies in the world remains an open question. There are serious reasons to question whether the primacy of the individual was a form of organization suited only to a particular time in the history of technology. Social media collapses independent thought, which can destroy the “wisdom of crowds”. AI provides an information aggregation decision-making mechanism that might one day eclipse markets, democracy, and other systems based on collections of independent human individuals.

It’s conceivable, in other words, that in the posthuman age, countries like America will not only lose their edge, but their whole reason for existence. Human rights, democracy, free enterprise, and so on might turn out to be ephemeral, transitional arrangements — shady pavilions under which intelligent life on Earth took a brief rest before continuing on to bigger and much weirder things. Or it might turn out that there are deep reasons, as yet poorly understood, why respect for the individual may always turn out to be the best way to organize society.

Regardless, I think it’s important that we acknowledge the singular importance and success of the United States of America during humanity’s most heroic age. It may last 1000 more years, or just 10, but it has already earned its place as the greatest country in history.

Share
